nativeRange.habitat: Caves and crevices of lagoon and seaward reefs and drop-offs, typically 8–42 m depth

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and crevices

diet.notes: Generally accepts vitamin-enriched frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, and finely chopped meaty foods. Has been documented foraging on tiny worms, copepods, and amphipods from live rock; a productive refugium or pod-populated tank is commonly considered beneficial for long-term success. Newly introduced specimens may need targeted feeding during acclimation.

summary: A tiny, secretive Indo-Pacific micro-wrasse with white banding and cryptic reef behavior. Generally considered reef-safe and well suited to peaceful nano reefs with abundant live rock crevices and a pod-rich refugium.
careNotes:
The White Banded Possum Wrasse (Wetmorella albofasciata) is a small, cryptic labrid closely related to the Yellow Banded Possum Wrasse (W. nigropinnata) but distinct in coloration and range. FishBase reports a maximum size of 6.0 cm TL; LiveAquaria lists 2.5 in (~6.35 cm) — the two are in close agreement, and a working adult range of about 5–6.5 cm is reasonable.

A minimum of around 75 L (20 gal) is commonly cited; this corresponds to the LiveAquaria stated minimum and matches the nearest nice tank-size value. The fish is reclusive on introduction, spending its first weeks deep in rockwork, and can struggle to compete for food with bolder tankmates. Aquascaping with abundant caves, overhangs, and shaded crevices is widely considered essential. A refugium supporting copepod and amphipod populations is commonly advised for long-term success.

This species is believed to be a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite, as is typical for wrasses generally. A single male can be kept with multiple females; sources advise introducing females before or simultaneously with the male to avoid aggression. Colors are reported to vary with mood and intensify during courtship.

Like most wrasses, this species has been known to jump, so a tight-fitting lid or fine mesh cover is generally advised. Once acclimated, it gradually becomes bolder and patrols the water column and cave network.

Cross-source notes: LiveAquaria temperature 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); FishBase wild temperature 24.2–28.2°C — averaged to 23–27°C for a captive-appropriate range. Size is consistent across sources at ~6 cm. Range: LiveAquaria states Indonesia; FishBase gives a much broader Indo-Pacific distribution from East Africa to Hawaii and the Society Islands.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of this species has not been reliably documented in available aquarium hobby sources. FishBase records distinct pairing during breeding in the wild. Spawning in home aquaria is generally considered unlikely outside of dedicated breeder setups.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Product page via wrasse-reef-safe collection. Provided adult size (2.5 in), minimum tank (10–20 gal, effectively 20 gal for species page), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), care level (Easy), reef-safe (yes), diet (carnivore; tiny worms, crustaceans, mysis, frozen, flake), native range (Indonesia), and notes on jumping and harem behavior.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Labridae / Eupercaria misc / Cheilininae), adult max size (6.0 cm TL), broader distribution (East Africa to Hawaiian and Society Islands, south to GBR; Micronesia), wild temperature (24.2–28.2°C), depth (8–42 m), habitat (caves/crevices, lagoon and seaward reefs), and reproduction (oviparous, distinct pairing). Trophic level 3.3. Conservation status Least Concern (2009).

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable captive-breeding accounts found in available hobby or scientific sources. FishBase records distinct pairing during wild spawning.
